title: Find trig values using angle addition identities
description: Find the trigonometric values of various angles that (while they are not special angles) can be expressed as the sum or difference of special angles and vice versa.
topic_path: Find trig values using angle addition identities

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\cos\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.**  
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{7\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the cosine addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\cos\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{7\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{7\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{7\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{12}}+\maroonE{\dfrac{4\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}+\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{7\pi}{12}$ is the sum of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final cosine value.*

###Evaluating $\cos\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the cosine addition identity, we get the following. 


$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\cos\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\cos\left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}+\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right) \\\\
&= \cos \left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) \cos \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right) - \sin \left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) \sin \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right) \\\\
&=\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) - \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\\\
&=\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{4}\right) - \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{4}\right)\\\\
&=\dfrac{\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{6}}{4}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\cos\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{6}}{4}$

options.alt: A unit circle in quadrant 1 with 5 points labeled along the circumference. The point at 0 radians is labeled 1, 0. The point at one-sixth pi radians is labeled square root of 3 halves, one-half. The point at one-fourth pi radians is labeled square root of 2 halves, square root of 2 halves. The point at one-third pi radians is labeled one-half, square root of 3 halves. The point at one-half pi radians is labeled 0, 1.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\cos\left(\dfrac{17\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.**  
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{17\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the cosine addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\cos\left(\dfrac{17\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{17\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{17\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{17\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{9\pi}{12}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{8\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{17\pi}{12}$ is the sum of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final cosine value.*

###Evaluating $\cos\left(\dfrac{17\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the cosine addition identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\cos\left(\dfrac{17\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\cos\left(\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}\right) \\\\
&= \cos \left(\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}\right) \cos \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}\right) - \sin \left(\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}\right) \sin \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}\right) \\\\
&=\left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \left( -\dfrac{1}{2} \right) - \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) \\\\
&=\left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{4} \right) - \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{4} \right)\\\\
&=\dfrac{\sqrt{2} - \sqrt{6}}{4}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\cos\left(\dfrac{17\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} - \sqrt{6}}{4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.**  
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the cosine addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{4\pi}{12}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{3\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{\pi}{12}$ is the difference of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final cosine value.*

###Evaluating $\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the cosine subtraction identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\cos\left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) \\\\
&= \cos \left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right) \cos \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) + \sin \left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right) \sin \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) \\\\
&=\left( \dfrac{1}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) + \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \\\\
&=\left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{4} \right) + \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{4} \right)\\\\
&=\dfrac{\sqrt{2} + \sqrt{6}}{4}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} + \sqrt{6}}{4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\cos\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.** 
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{13\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the cosine addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\cos\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{13\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{13\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{13\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{16\pi}{12}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{3\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{4\pi}{3}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{13\pi}{12}$ is the difference of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{4\pi}{3}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final cosine value.*

###Evaluating $\cos\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the cosine subtraction identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\cos\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\cos\left(\blueE{\dfrac{4\pi}{3}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) \\\\
&= \cos \left(\blueE{\dfrac{4\pi}{3}}\right) \cos \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) + \sin \left(\blueE{\dfrac{4\pi}{3}}\right) \sin \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) \\\\
&=\left( -\dfrac{1}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) + \left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \\\\
&=\left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{4} \right) + \left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{4} \right)\\\\
&=\dfrac{-\sqrt{2} - \sqrt{6}}{4}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\cos\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{-\sqrt{2} - \sqrt{6}}{4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following expression:

$\cos \left(\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) - \sin \left(\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)$

**Find the exact value of the expression without a calculator.**  
[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

The expression matches the pattern for one of the angle addition formulas.

Once we figure out which pattern exactly, we can rewrite the expression as the trigonometric value (either sine, cosine, or tangent) of the sum or difference of $\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ and $\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This should hopefully end up as the trigonometric value of a special angle, which we can then find exactly.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

### Choosing an identity

The expression matches the pattern for $\cos (\phi + \theta)$ where $\phi=\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$.

###Finding the combined angle and evaluating

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\cos \left(\dfrac{3\pi}{4} \right) \cos \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) - \sin \left(\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)
\\\\
&= \cos\left( \dfrac{3\pi}{4} + \dfrac{5\pi}{12} \right) \\\\
&= \cos\left( \dfrac{9\pi}{12} + \dfrac{5\pi}{12} \right)\\\\
&= \cos\left( \dfrac{14\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&= -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Summary

$\cos \left(\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) - \sin \left(\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)=-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following expression:

$\cos \left(\dfrac{7\pi}{4}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) - \sin \left(\dfrac{7\pi}{4}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)$

**Find the exact value of the expression without a calculator.**  
[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

The expression matches the pattern for one of the angle addition formulas.

Once we figure out which pattern exactly, we can rewrite the expression as the trigonometric value (either sine, cosine, or tangent) of the sum or difference of $\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$ and $\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This should hopefully end up as the trigonometric value of a special angle, which we can then find exactly.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

### Choosing an identity

The expression matches the pattern for $\cos (\phi + \theta)$ where $\phi=\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$.

###Finding the combined angle and evaluating

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\cos \left(\dfrac{7\pi}{4} \right) \cos \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) - \sin \left(\dfrac{7\pi}{4}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)
\\\\
&= \cos\left( \dfrac{7\pi}{4} + \dfrac{5\pi}{12} \right) \\\\
&= \cos\left( \dfrac{21\pi}{12} + \dfrac{5\pi}{12} \right)\\\\
&= \cos\left( \dfrac{26\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&= \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Summary

$\cos \left(\dfrac{7\pi}{4}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) - \sin \left(\dfrac{7\pi}{4}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following expression:

$\cos \left(\dfrac{11\pi}{12}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) + \sin \left(\dfrac{11\pi}{12}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$

**Find the exact value of the expression without a calculator.**  
[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

The expression matches the pattern for one of the angle addition formulas.

Once we figure out which pattern exactly, we can rewrite the expression as the trigonometric value (either sine, cosine, or tangent) of the sum or difference of $\dfrac{11\pi}{12}$ and $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This should hopefully end up as the trigonometric value of a special angle, which we can then find exactly.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

### Choosing an identity

The expression matches the pattern for $\cos (\phi - \theta)$ where $\phi=\dfrac{11\pi}{12}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$.

###Finding the combined angle and evaluating

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\cos \left(\dfrac{11\pi}{12} \right) \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) + \sin \left(\dfrac{11\pi}{12}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)
\\\\
&= \cos\left( \dfrac{11\pi}{12} - \dfrac{\pi}{6} \right) \\\\
&= \cos\left( \dfrac{11\pi}{12} - \dfrac{2\pi}{12} \right)\\\\
&= \cos\left( \dfrac{9\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&= -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Summary

$\cos \left(\dfrac{11\pi}{12}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) + \sin \left(\dfrac{11\pi}{12}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.**  
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the sine addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{5\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{8\pi}{12}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{3\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$ is the difference of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final sine value.*

###Evaluating $\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the sine subtraction identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\sin\left(\blueE{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) \\\\
&= \sin \left(\blueE{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}\right) \cos \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) - \cos \left(\blueE{\dfrac{2\pi}{3}}\right) \sin \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) \\\\
&=\left( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) - \left( -\dfrac{1}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \\\\
&=\left( \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{4} \right) - \left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{4} \right)\\\\
&=\dfrac{\sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{4}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\sin\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.**  
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{13\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the sine addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\sin\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{13\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{13\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{13\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{9\pi}{12}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{4\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{13\pi}{12}$ is the sum of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final sine value.*

###Evaluating $\sin\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the sine addition identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\sin\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\sin\left(\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right) \\\\
&= \sin \left(\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}\right) \cos \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right) + \cos \left(\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}\right) \sin \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right) \\\\
&=\left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{1}{2} \right) + \left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) \\\\
&=\left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{4} \right) + \left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{4} \right)\\\\
&=\dfrac{\sqrt{2} - \sqrt{6}}{4}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\sin\left(\dfrac{13\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} - \sqrt{6}}{4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\sin\left(\dfrac{19\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.**  
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{19\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the sine addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\sin\left(\dfrac{19\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{19\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{19\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{19\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{21\pi}{12}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{2\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{19\pi}{12}$ is the difference of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final sine value.*

###Evaluating $\sin\left(\dfrac{19\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the sine subtraction identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\sin\left(\dfrac{19\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\sin\left(\blueE{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right) \\\\
&= \sin \left(\blueE{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}}\right) \cos \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right) - \cos \left(\blueE{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}}\right) \sin \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right) \\\\
&=\left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) - \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{1}{2} \right) \\\\
&=\left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{4} \right) - \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{4} \right)\\\\
&=\dfrac{-\sqrt{6} - \sqrt{2}}{4}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\sin\left(\dfrac{19\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{-\sqrt{6} - \sqrt{2}}{4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\sin\left(\dfrac{23\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.**  
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{23\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the sine addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\sin\left(\dfrac{23\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{23\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{23\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{23\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{12}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{20\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{5\pi}{3}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{23\pi}{12}$ is the sum of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{5\pi}{3}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final sine value.*

###Evaluating $\sin\left(\dfrac{23\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the sine addition identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\sin\left(\dfrac{23\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\sin\left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{5\pi}{3}}\right) \\\\
&= \sin \left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) \cos \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{5\pi}{3}}\right) + \cos \left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) \sin \left(\maroonE{\dfrac{5\pi}{3}}\right) \\\\
&=\left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \left( \dfrac{1}{2} \right) + \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) \left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) \\\\
&=\left( \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{4} \right) + \left( -\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{4} \right)\\\\
&=\dfrac{\sqrt{2} - \sqrt{6}}{4}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\sin\left(\dfrac{23\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} - \sqrt{6}}{4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following expression:

$\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) + \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right)$

**Find the exact value of the expression without a calculator.**  
[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

The expression matches the pattern for one of the angle addition formulas.

Once we figure out which pattern exactly, we can rewrite the expression as the trigonometric value (either sine, cosine, or tangent) of the sum or difference of $\dfrac{\pi}{8}$ and $\dfrac{\pi}{8}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This should hopefully end up as the trigonometric value of a special angle, which we can then find exactly.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

### Choosing an identity

The expression matches the pattern for $\sin (\phi + \theta)$ where $\phi=\dfrac{\pi}{8}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{8}$.

###Finding the combined angle and evaluating

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8} \right) \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) + \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right)
\\\\
&= \sin\left( \dfrac{\pi}{8} + \dfrac{\pi}{8} \right) \\\\
&= \sin\left( \dfrac{2\pi}{8}\right)\\\\
&= \sin\left( \dfrac{\pi}{4} \right)\\\\
&= \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}
\end{align}$

### Summary

$\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) + \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right)=\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following expression:

$\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) - \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)$

**Find the exact value of the expression without a calculator.**  
[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

The expression matches the pattern for one of the angle addition formulas.

Once we figure out which pattern exactly, we can rewrite the expression as the trigonometric value (either sine, cosine, or tangent) of the sum or difference of $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ and $\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This should hopefully end up as the trigonometric value of a special angle, which we can then find exactly.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

### Choosing an identity

The expression matches the pattern for $\sin (\phi - \theta)$ where $\phi=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$.

###Finding the combined angle and evaluating

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6} \right) \cos \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) - \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)
\\\\
&= \sin\left( \dfrac{\pi}{6} - \dfrac{5\pi}{12} \right) \\\\
&= \sin\left( \dfrac{2\pi}{12} - \dfrac{5\pi}{12} \right)\\\\
&= \sin\left( -\dfrac{3\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&= -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Summary

$\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) - \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)=-\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$

$\sin \left(\dfrac{9\pi}{8}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) - \cos \left(\dfrac{9\pi}{8}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right)$

**Find the exact value of the expression without a calculator.**  
[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

The expression matches the pattern for one of the angle addition formulas.

Once we figure out which pattern exactly, we can rewrite the expression as the trigonometric value (either sine, cosine, or tangent) of the sum or difference of $\dfrac{9\pi}{8}$ and $\dfrac{\pi}{8}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This should hopefully end up as the trigonometric value of a special angle, which we can then find exactly.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

### Choosing an identity

The expression matches the pattern for $\sin (\phi - \theta)$ where $\phi=\dfrac{9\pi}{8}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{8}$.

###Finding the combined angle and evaluating

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\sin \left(\dfrac{9\pi}{8} \right) \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) - \cos \left(\dfrac{9\pi}{8}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right)
\\\\
&= \sin\left( \dfrac{9\pi}{8} - \dfrac{\pi}{8} \right) \\\\
&= \sin\left( \dfrac{8\pi}{8}\right)\\\\
&= 0
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Summary

$\sin \left(\dfrac{9\pi}{8}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right) - \cos \left(\dfrac{9\pi}{8}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}\right)=0$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.**  
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the tangent addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{12}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{2\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{\pi}{12}$ is the difference of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final tangent value.*

###Evaluating $\tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the tangent subtraction identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\tan\left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right) \\\\
&= \dfrac{\tan\left( \blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) - \tan\left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}} \right)}{1 + \tan\left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} \right) \tan\left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}} \right)}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\left( 1\right) - \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \right)}{1 + \left(1 \right) \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \right)}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\left(1 - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right) }{\left(1 +\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \right)}\cdot \dfrac{3}{3}\\\\
&= \dfrac{3-\sqrt{3}}{3+\sqrt{3}}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{3-\sqrt{3}}{3+\sqrt{3}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.** 
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{7\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the tangent addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{7\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{7\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{7\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{12}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{4\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{7\pi}{12}$ is the sum of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final tangent value.*

###Evaluating $\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the tangent addition identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\tan\left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}}\right) \\\\
&= \dfrac{\tan\left( \blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) + \tan\left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}} \right)}{1 - \tan\left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} \right) \tan\left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{3}} \right)}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\left( 1\right) + \left(\sqrt{3} \right)}{1 - \left(1 \right) \left(\sqrt{3} \right)}\\\\
&= \dfrac{1+\sqrt{3}}{1-\sqrt{3}}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{1+\sqrt{3}}{1-\sqrt{3}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\tan\left(\dfrac{19\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.**  
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{19\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the tangent addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\tan\left(\dfrac{19\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{19\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{19\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{19\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{21\pi}{12}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{2\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{19\pi}{12}$ is the difference of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final tangent value.*

###Evaluating $\tan\left(\dfrac{19\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the tangent subtraction identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\tan\left(\dfrac{19\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\tan\left(\blueE{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}} - \maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}\right) \\\\
&= \dfrac{\tan\left( \blueE{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}}\right) - \tan\left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}} \right)}{1 + \tan\left(\blueE{\dfrac{7\pi}{4}} \right) \tan\left(\maroonE{\dfrac{\pi}{6}} \right)}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\left( -1\right) - \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \right)}{1 + \left(-1 \right) \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \right)}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\left( -1 - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \right)}{\left(1 - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \right)}\cdot \dfrac{-3}{-3}\\\\
&= \dfrac{3+\sqrt{3}}{-3+\sqrt{3}}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\tan\left(\dfrac{19\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{3+\sqrt{3}}{-3+\sqrt{3}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find $\tan\left(\dfrac{23\pi}{12}\right)$ exactly using an angle addition or subtraction formula.**  
*[[☃ explanation 1]]*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy 

First, we should rewrite the given angle $\dfrac{23\pi}{12}$ as the sum or difference of two special angles.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Then, we can use the tangent addition or subtraction identities in order to evaluate $\tan\left(\dfrac{23\pi}{12}\right)$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Rewriting $\dfrac{23\pi}{12}$ 

We can rewrite $\dfrac{23\pi}{12}$ as follows. 

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{23\pi}{12}&=\blueE{\dfrac{3\pi}{12}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{20\pi}{12}}\\\\
&=\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{5\pi}{3}}
\end{align}$ 

In other words, $\dfrac{23\pi}{12}$ is the sum of the special angles $\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}$ and $\maroonE{\dfrac{5\pi}{3}}$.

*Note: There are other pairs of angles we could add or subtract to get the given angle. Any pair will give us the same final tangent value.*

###Evaluating $\tan\left(\dfrac{23\pi}{12}\right)$ 

Using the tangent addition identity, we get the following. 

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\tan\left(\dfrac{23\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&=
\tan\left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} + \maroonE{\dfrac{5\pi}{3}}\right) \\\\
&= \dfrac{\tan\left( \blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\right) + \tan\left(\maroonE{\dfrac{5\pi}{3}} \right)}{1 - \tan\left(\blueE{\dfrac{\pi}{4}} \right) \tan\left(\maroonE{\dfrac{5\pi}{3}} \right)}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\left( 1\right) + \left(-\sqrt{3} \right)}{1 - \left(1 \right) \left(-\sqrt{3} \right)}\\\\
&= \dfrac{1-\sqrt{3}}{1+\sqrt{3}}
\end{align}$

###Summary 

$\tan\left(\dfrac{23\pi}{12}\right) = \dfrac{1-\sqrt{3}}{1+\sqrt{3}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following expression:

$\dfrac{\tan\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{4}\right)+\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)}{1-\tan\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{4}\right)\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)}$

**Find the exact value of the expression without a calculator.**  
[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

The expression matches the pattern for one of the angle addition formulas.

Once we figure out which pattern exactly, we can rewrite the expression as the trigonometric value (either sine, cosine, or tangent) of the sum or difference of $\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$ and $\dfrac{7\pi}{12}$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

This should hopefully end up as the trigonometric value of a special angle, which we can then find exactly.

[[☃ explanation 3]]

### Choosing an identity

The expression matches the pattern for $\tan(\phi+\theta)$ where $\phi=\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{7\pi}{12}$.

###Finding the combined angle and evaluating

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{\tan\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{4}\right)+\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)}{1-\tan\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{4}\right)\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)}\\\\
&=\tan\left( \dfrac{5\pi}{4}+\dfrac{7\pi}{12} \right) \\\\
&=\tan\left( \dfrac{15\pi}{12}+\dfrac{7\pi}{12} \right) \\\\
&=\tan\left(\dfrac{22\pi}{12} \right) \\\\
&= -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Summary

$\dfrac{\tan\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{4}\right)+\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)}{1-\tan\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{4}\right)\tan\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{12}\right)} = -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following expression:

$\dfrac{\tan\left( \dfrac{\pi}{12} \right) + \tan\left( \dfrac{5\pi}{3} \right)}{1 - \tan\left( \dfrac{\pi}{12} \right)\tan\left( \dfrac{5\pi}{3} \right)}$

**Find the exact value of the expression without a calculator.**  
[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

The expression matches the pattern for one of the angle addition formulas.

Once we figure out which pattern exactly, we can rewrite the expression as the trigonometric value (either sine, cosine, or tangent) of the sum or difference of $\dfrac{\pi}{12}$ and $\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

This should hopefully end up as the trigonometric value of a special angle, which we can then find exactly.

[[☃ explanation 3]]

### Choosing an identity

The expression matches the pattern for $\tan (\phi + \theta)$ where $\phi=\dfrac{\pi}{12}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$.

###Finding the combined angle and evaluating

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{\tan\left( \dfrac{\pi}{12} \right) + \tan\left( \dfrac{5\pi}{3} \right)}{1 - \tan\left( \dfrac{\pi}{12} \right)\tan\left( \dfrac{5\pi}{3} \right)}\\\\
&= \tan\left( \dfrac{\pi}{12} + \dfrac{5\pi}{3} \right) \\\\
&= \tan\left( \dfrac{\pi}{12} + \dfrac{20\pi}{12} \right)\\\\
&= \tan\left( \dfrac{21\pi}{12}\right)\\\\
&= -1
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Summary

$\dfrac{\tan\left( \dfrac{\pi}{12} \right) + \tan\left( \dfrac{5\pi}{3} \right)}{1 - \tan\left( \dfrac{\pi}{12} \right)\tan\left( \dfrac{5\pi}{3} \right)} = -1$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following expression:

$\dfrac{\tan\left( \dfrac{25\pi}{24} \right) - \tan\left( \dfrac{3\pi}{8} \right)}{1 + \tan\left( \dfrac{25\pi}{24} \right)\tan\left( \dfrac{3\pi}{8} \right)}$

**Find the exact value of the expression without a calculator.**  
[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

The expression matches the pattern for one of the angle addition formulas.

Once we figure out which pattern exactly, we can rewrite the expression as the trigonometric value (either sine, cosine, or tangent) of the sum or difference of $\dfrac{25\pi}{24}$ and $\dfrac{3\pi}{8}$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

This should hopefully end up as the trigonometric value of a special angle, which we can then find exactly.

[[☃ explanation 3]]

### Choosing an identity

The expression matches the pattern for $\tan (\phi - \theta)$ where $\phi=\dfrac{25\pi}{24}$ and $\theta=\dfrac{3\pi}{8}$.

###Finding the combined angle and evaluating

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{\tan\left( \dfrac{25\pi}{24} \right) - \tan\left( \dfrac{3\pi}{8} \right)}{1 + \tan\left( \dfrac{25\pi}{24} \right)\tan\left( \dfrac{3\pi}{8} \right)}\\\\
&= \tan\left( \dfrac{25\pi}{24} - \dfrac{3\pi}{8} \right) \\\\
&= \tan\left( \dfrac{25\pi}{24} - \dfrac{9\pi}{24} \right)\\\\
&= \tan\left( \dfrac{16\pi}{24}\right)\\\\
&= -\sqrt{3}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Summary

$\dfrac{\tan\left( \dfrac{25\pi}{24} \right) - \tan\left( \dfrac{3\pi}{8} \right)}{1 + \tan\left( \dfrac{25\pi}{24} \right)\tan\left( \dfrac{3\pi}{8} \right)} = -\sqrt{3}$
